nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef caves, overhangs, and shaded reef walls where light is low and water flow is strong; often found in cryptic reef habitats sheltered from direct sunlight

decorPreferences: cave or overhang placement away from direct light

diet.notes: Entirely non-photosynthetic (azooxanthellate) — relies completely on target feeding and ambient plankton. Daily spot-feeding with a mixture of phytoplankton, zooplankton (rotifers, copepods), and fine-particle foods is generally considered essential. Sources suggest turning off return pumps during spot feeding to allow polyps to capture particles. A refugium or live phytoplankton reactor producing continuous planktonic input tends to improve long-term outcomes.

goodWith: other NPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: large polyp corals that may overgrow
high-light corals in same placement zone
fast-growing encrusting corals near the base

summary: Distichopora (Lace Coral) is a hydrozoan — not a true coral — sold in the reef trade for its delicate, colorful branching skeleton. Azooxanthellate; requires daily feeding and shade. Expert-level NPS care.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT TAXONOMIC NOTE: Distichopora is sold and traded as a coral but is biologically a hydrozoan (Class Hydrozoa, Order Anthoathecata, Family Stylasteridae) — more closely related to jellyfish and Millepora fire coral than to stony or soft corals. With approximately 34 described species, it is listed in the trade at genus level (Distichopora sp.) since multiple species share similar aquarium husbandry requirements. Common species offered include D. violacea (purple/blue), D. nitida, and various orange and red forms.

NPS (NON-PHOTOSYNTHETIC) CARE — CRITICAL: Unlike most traded corals, Distichopora contains no symbiotic zooxanthellae whatsoever. It derives zero energy from light and depends entirely on capturing suspended particulate food from the water column. This makes it one of the most demanding corals in the hobby. Hobbyists who have reported any success generally attribute it to: (1) dedicated NPS systems with constant planktonic food supply, (2) refugia producing live phyto- and zooplankton, and (3) daily spot-feeding with a range of particle sizes. Several experienced sources note a near-zero long-term survival rate in typical reef aquariums, as the species quickly starves without adequate particulate food delivery.

STING: Distichopora possesses cnidocytes (stinging cells) capable of mild irritation on skin contact. The sting is generally considered significantly lighter than Millepora fire coral — most aquarists describe it as a minor nuisance rather than a medical concern — but sensitive individuals may have stronger reactions. Gloves are advisable when handling.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Low light is strongly recommended — 30–100 PAR. Being azooxanthellate, it has no photosynthetic requirement and excess light reportedly bleaches the colony and promotes competing algae or cyanobacterial growth on the skeleton. Placement under overhangs, in cave-like shaded areas, or in lower-light parts of the tank is generally advised.
• Flow: Strong, consistent water movement is considered essential to continuously deliver food particles to the polyps and prevent detritus accumulation on the skeleton. Sources generally describe medium-to-high flow as appropriate, with some recommending placement in a high-flow corridor.
• Placement: Anywhere with adequate strong flow and shade; mounting upside-down under a ledge or overhang is considered ideal, mimicking natural cave-roof habitat.
• Skeleton maintenance: The lacy calcium carbonate skeleton is extremely brittle — fragging is difficult and even minor handling risks breakage. The skeleton must be kept free of diatoms and cyanobacterial slime, which can smother tissue. Gentle flow directed across the colony tends to help.
• Feeding: Daily target feeding with microplankton is generally recommended. Turning off powerheads for 10–15 minutes during spot feeding reportedly improves food capture. A variety of particle sizes — phytoplankton, rotifers, copepods, oyster eggs, and fine frozen zooplankton — have been documented as suitable foods.
• Water chemistry: Despite being non-photosynthetic, Distichopora deposits a calcareous skeleton and reportedly benefits from maintained calcium (~400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–11 dKH), and magnesium (~1260–1350 ppm), similar to other calcifying cnidarians.
• Nocturnal polyp extension: Polyps are reported to extend most fully at night; target feeding after lights-out is generally considered more effective.

CITES: Stylasteridae (lace corals) may be subject to collection and trade restrictions depending on the specific species and jurisdiction. Verifying provenance of any purchased specimen is advisable.

Capture and transport survival for this genus is historically poor; specimens that appear healthy in the store may already be in decline. A well-established, nutrient-rich NPS tank running for at least 6 months is generally considered a prerequisite.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Distichopora has not been documented in the hobby. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Asexual propagation by fragging is theoretically possible but is reported to be extremely difficult given the skeleton's fragility — any cut tends to shatter rather than cleave cleanly. No reliable captive fragging or propagation protocol has been established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Lace Stick Coral, Purple (Expert Only)
primary.notes: Direct species page found via LiveAquaria site search for 'distichopora'. Provided care level (Expert Only), temperament (Peaceful), temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, salinity SG 1.023-1.025, kH 8-12, low lighting, medium-to-strong flow, middle placement, NPS feeding requirements with microplankton.

additional.name: FreshMarine – Distichopora Assorted Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 72-78°F (22-26°C), pH 8.10-8.40, salinity SG 1.023-1.025, low lighting, moderate flow, planktivore diet (zooplankton + phytoplankton), calcium/iodine/strontium/trace supplementation noted. Also confirmed azooxanthellate hydrocoral biology and nocturnal polyp extension.

additional.name: ReefTankResource – Non-Photosynthetic (NPS) Corals Guide
additional.notes: NPS-specific parameters: temperature 72-76°F (22-24°C), salinity 1.023-1.026, pH 7.8-8.2, calcium 420-440 ppm, alkalinity 8-9.5 dKH, magnesium 1260-1350 ppm, PAR 50-150 for NPS. Daily feeding with diverse particle sizes recommended. Distichopora listed among hardest corals to keep.

additional.name: Melev's Reef – Distichopora Coral
additional.notes: Confirmed 34 nominal species, azooxanthellate biology, cave-roof habitat preference, need for phyto/zooplankton feeding, slime-free skeleton maintenance. Notes near-zero captive survival without proper NPS husbandry. Family Stylasteridae, Class Hydrozoa.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Distichopora (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: Family Stylasteridae, Order Anthoathecata, Class Hydrozoa. Native range confirmed as Pacific, Indian, and Central Atlantic Oceans. Established by Lamarck 1816. Not used for husbandry parameters.
